The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

b6cd1640-fdc3-4525-bbb4-f148699876df.fact: The Major Deegan Expressway (I-87) is named for William F. Deegan, a Bronx politician and civic booster of the 1920s who never lived to see a highway named after him.
Running alongside the Harlem River, the Deegan connects the Bronx to Midtown via the Triborough Bridge — a route so important that delays here cascade across the entire metro area.
The stretch near Yankee Stadium is one of the most recognizable game-day traffic jams in America — the highway essentially becomes a parking lot before every home game.

b6558fc0-d62b-4b25-b7aa-6b592bde2c44.fact: Flatbush Avenue follows the path of an ancient Lenape trail, later the main road through the Dutch colonial town of Flatbush — one of Brooklyn's original six Dutch settlements.
The avenue runs 11 miles from the Manhattan Bridge to Floyd Bennett Field at Jamaica Bay, cutting diagonally across Brooklyn's grid in a way that creates dozens of awkward intersections.

b0670166-0aa0-4f93-a6f6-8bf07a9ea663.fact: The St. George neighborhood at Staten Island's northern tip is undergoing a revival — the ferry terminal, a minor league baseball stadium, and the Snug Harbor Cultural Center are drawing visitors who have never explored the borough before.

The Staten Island Ferry is free and has been since 1997 — 70,000 riders a day make the crossing for work or pleasure, with one of the best views of the Statue of Liberty and Lower Manhattan skyline of any transit ride in the world.

789b70ea-f0f9-4e61-b72d-e4bf79fd2218.fact: The Clearview Expressway is one of Queens's least-known highways — a north-south connector that quietly links the LIE to the Whitestone Bridge without passing through major commercial areas.
The expressway skirts Cunningham Park and Alley Pond Park, two of Queens's largest green spaces — you can catch glimpses of forested hillsides from the highway in an otherwise dense urban area.

43bb4857-e86e-4775-916e-6b26cedaf554.fact: Harlem became an African-American neighborhood almost by accident — a 1900s real estate crash left buildings empty, and Black families moved in from the overcrowded Tenderloin district downtown.

The Apollo Theater on 125th Street launched careers including James Brown, Ella Fitzgerald, and Michael Jackson — its amateur night, running since 1934, remains one of NYC's most electric live events.

Simon & Garfunkel immortalized it as the '59th Street Bridge' in their 1966 hit 'Feelin' Groovy' — locals still call it that.
It's a cantilever bridge, not suspension — the two steel towers support the deck from above rather than hanging it from cables.
Roosevelt Island sits in the middle of the East River beneath the bridge, accessible by a Swiss-made aerial tramway that's been running since 1976.

ba4a1b3a-6e33-4742-a471-18f204f488ef.fact: Williamsburg's transformation from industrial waterfront to hipster capital was so dramatic it became a sociological phenomenon — the neighborhood is credited with exporting the 'artisanal' aesthetic to cities worldwide.

Before its 2000s reinvention, Williamsburg's waterfront held enormous industrial operations — the Domino Sugar refinery on the East River processed more sugar than anywhere in the world in the early 20th century.

bc2fd2da-1d56-46fa-a1ab-8674fac93877.fact: Times Square was called Longacre Square until 1904, when the New York Times moved its headquarters here and convinced the city to rename it — and add a New Year's Eve ball drop.
At peak times, Times Square hosts 380,000 pedestrians per day — making it one of the most foot-trafficked intersections on the planet.
The famous billboards are technically 'spectaculars' in advertising industry jargon — Times Square has more square footage of signage than almost anywhere on Earth.

45756112-04f9-429f-9754-1939e25f4184.fact: The Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883 as the longest suspension bridge on Earth — and the first to use steel wire cables instead of iron.
Chief engineer Washington Roebling directed construction from his bedroom window through binoculars after being crippled by decompression sickness, while his wife Emily supervised on-site.
P.T. Barnum marched 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove it was structurally sound to skeptical New Yorkers — it worked.
About 10,000 pedestrians and cyclists cross the bridge's elevated walkway every day, making it one of NYC's most beloved public spaces.

140a20d7-7ae7-409c-8c18-67d6b2ef7b2d.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

cb94f806-6014-496b-960b-9f747109980e.fact: The Rockaways peninsula is NYC's most unusual geography — a long barrier beach accessible by subway, where city residents surf, lifeguard, and maintain beach bungalows within the five boroughs.
The Rockaways were a major resort destination in the early 1900s, packed with hotels and bathhouses — Hurricane Sandy in 2012 devastated the community, spurring a remarkable rebuilding effort.

45cb119b-0e4a-442e-9410-b810ab8c255d.fact: Times Square was called Longacre Square until 1904, when the New York Times moved its headquarters here and convinced the city to rename it — and add a New Year's Eve ball drop.

454e566c-e3e3-4426-b91a-e0ea709e70f7.fact: The Staten Island Expressway (I-278) is the main east-west artery of the island — it was supposed to connect to an outer belt highway that was never built, leaving it as a stub.
Construction in the 1950s and 60s required demolishing hundreds of homes in Sunnyside and other mid-island neighborhoods, following the same Moses playbook used in Brooklyn.

a6b2c1c8-1124-4dec-aa22-c8d87f6835ec.fact: The West Shore Expressway runs alongside the Arthur Kill waterway separating Staten Island from New Jersey — a tidal strait so industrialized it was once called 'the most polluted body of water in the world.'
The land along the West Shore was home to oyster beds that supplied Manhattan restaurants until industrial pollution killed the beds in the early 20th century.

b43f6602-fb0f-4eb4-a019-ac7101aab067.fact: Brooklyn Heights was the first NYC neighborhood designated a historic district (1965) — its Federal and Greek Revival brownstones, built by wealthy Manhattan merchants in the 1820s-40s, are some of the finest examples of 19th-century domestic architecture in the US.
The Brooklyn Heights Promenade offers what many consider the best skyline view in New York — the iconic vista of Lower Manhattan across the East River has appeared in countless films and photographs.

The neighborhood's St. Marks Place has been famous for cheap, diverse food since the 1970s — Japanese curry, Ukrainian pierogies, and NYC pizza slices still coexist within a few blocks.

4589cc19-b5b5-4696-a199-20f753ec74c4.fact: The Grand Central Parkway was one of Robert Moses's first major projects, opening in 1936 as a showcase of modern highway design — wide lanes, gentle curves, and landscaped margins.
The parkway provides the primary access route to LaGuardia Airport, meaning its traffic level shifts dramatically with flight schedules.
It runs through Cunningham Park and Alley Pond Park, preserving one of Queens's largest stretches of old-growth forest right alongside a busy expressway.

4be502c3-830e-40d3-8c39-1047d50adc9c.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.
The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

43a4279e-de3a-4ab3-8c86-b268eb5e8848.fact: Flatbush Avenue follows the path of an ancient Lenape trail, later the main road through the Dutch colonial town of Flatbush — one of Brooklyn's original six Dutch settlements.
The avenue runs 11 miles from the Manhattan Bridge to Floyd Bennett Field at Jamaica Bay, cutting diagonally across Brooklyn's grid in a way that creates dozens of awkward intersections.

bca4a4b0-d73f-4937-9301-85ff8293bd94.fact: Times Square was called Longacre Square until 1904, when the New York Times moved its headquarters here and convinced the city to rename it — and add a New Year's Eve ball drop.

The famous billboards are technically 'spectaculars' in advertising industry jargon — Times Square has more square footage of signage than almost anywhere on Earth.

4850e464-1111-4b5d-a72d-f54a0e12a789.fact: Midtown Manhattan is the engine of the global economy — on any weekday, more business decisions are made per square mile here than almost anywhere on Earth.
The subway system beneath these streets carries more passengers per year than any other rapid transit system in the Western Hemisphere — over a billion rides annually.
Most New Yorkers would rather walk three blocks in a snowstorm than wait for a Midtown cab at rush hour — and they're usually right about the math.

4f688c33-672c-4bac-bf2b-98438d464ccd.fact: The Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883 as the longest suspension bridge on Earth — and the first to use steel wire cables instead of iron.
Chief engineer Washington Roebling directed construction from his bedroom window through binoculars after being crippled by decompression sickness, while his wife Emily supervised on-site.
P.T. Barnum marched 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove it was structurally sound to skeptical New Yorkers — it worked.
About 10,000 pedestrians and cyclists cross the bridge's elevated walkway every day, making it one of NYC's most beloved public spaces.

1a626331-3c36-48bd-91a5-edd930c314c0.fact: The Major Deegan Expressway (I-87) is named for William F. Deegan, a Bronx politician and civic booster of the 1920s who never lived to see a highway named after him.
Running alongside the Harlem River, the Deegan connects the Bronx to Midtown via the Triborough Bridge — a route so important that delays here cascade across the entire metro area.
The stretch near Yankee Stadium is one of the most recognizable game-day traffic jams in America — the highway essentially becomes a parking lot before every home game.

1b874f5c-e1c6-481d-bb85-8d7d92c1793b.fact: Crown Heights is where the Great Migration brought Caribbean communities to Brooklyn — the neighborhood is a living mosaic of Haitian, Trinidadian, Jamaican, and Barbadian culture alongside long-established Jewish communities.
The Brooklyn Museum, on Eastern Parkway, holds one of the largest art collections in the United States — it was actually planned to be bigger than the Met before funding ran out in 1927.

50725dbd-6e39-4ed0-aa3b-89f9c8feff4e.fact: The FDR Drive runs 9.5 miles along Manhattan's east shore, built largely on landfill created from subway tunnel excavation and Depression-era public works.
Originally called the East River Drive, it was renamed for Franklin D. Roosevelt after his death in 1945 — a fitting tribute to the president who championed public works.
It's technically a parkway, so trucks are banned — the low clearance underpasses were a deliberate design choice to exclude commercial vehicles.

bfeb2434-fd55-4024-8172-f9602ea6ae06.fact: The Hutchinson River Parkway is named for Anne Hutchinson, the religious dissenter who settled the Bronx-Westchester area in 1642 after being banished from Massachusetts Bay Colony.
The parkway follows a tidal estuary through Pelham Bay Park — one of the few places in the city where you can watch herons fishing from the highway median.

70c286f0-78bc-43e4-9284-63ea2843c769.fact: Brighton Beach is known as 'Little Odessa' — a large Russian-speaking community settled here after the Soviet Union's collapse, filling the boardwalk with Russian restaurants, nightclubs, and grocery stores.

The Brighton Beach neighborhood has its own microclimate — the ocean keeps it cooler in summer and warmer in winter than most of Brooklyn, which is why early resort developers chose this spot in the 1860s.

66533d67-7fe3-4250-b850-88c2fd7ec0e0.fact: Father Capodanno Boulevard is named for Vincent Capodanno, a Staten Island-born Navy chaplain killed in Vietnam in 1967 while protecting wounded Marines — he was posthumously awarded the Medal of Honor.
The boulevard runs along the Staten Island beachfront at South Beach, one of the borough's few public beaches — local families have been coming here since the 1920s.

547cd268-58f9-4a84-a235-dbaa0432d79a.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

0bcfbc92-d455-4f62-846a-32afbefa3b4b.fact: The Upper West Side has been NYC's intellectual and artistic neighborhood since the 1960s — Zabar's deli, the 72nd Street subway, and Riverside Park are the anchors of the local identity.
Broadway on the Upper West Side retains its diagonal path from the original Bloomingdale Road, creating triangular 'squares' like Verdi Square at 72nd and Straus Park at 106th.

50fed16e-8068-4b89-ada4-246fc112b924.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.
The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

7c92c63b-2786-4600-9108-62cea2806345.fact: The LIE earned its nickname 'the world's longest parking lot' so thoroughly that traffic reporters have their own lore about which stretch is moving today.
Construction began in 1937 and continued piecemeal for three decades as Queens suburbanized — you can see the highway's character shift from urban to suburban as you go east.
At peak hours, the LIE carries over 200,000 vehicles per day through Queens — one of the most congested stretches of highway in the United States.

53de1e7c-8443-47fa-8603-4e5ef0466c17.fact: Queens Boulevard earned the nickname 'the Boulevard of Death' — between 1990 and 1998, 186 pedestrians were killed trying to cross its 12 lanes, spurring a massive safety redesign.
The boulevard runs 7.7 miles from the East River to the Nassau County border, passing through a dozen distinct Queens neighborhoods — essentially a cross-section of the entire borough.

bff9cee2-1513-4af3-9838-ea7a35552049.fact: Rockefeller Center was built during the Depression (1930-1939) — John D. Rockefeller Jr. lost millions but created 70,000 construction jobs when New York needed them most.
The Top of the Rock observation deck offers arguably the best view of Central Park in the city — and unlike the Empire State, you can see the Empire State from here.
The complex has its own zip code (10112), its own private street (Rockefeller Plaza), and an underground concourse connecting 19 buildings.

513234e1-ea04-47bc-b607-c6ff1bb6a18d.fact: The LIE earned its nickname 'the world's longest parking lot' so thoroughly that traffic reporters have their own lore about which stretch is moving today.

d1363c77-e061-4414-be82-e6d749254859.fact: Grand Central Terminal opened in 1913 with the world's largest train shed — the main concourse ceiling famously shows a painted constellation map, though it's reversed from the actual sky.
Jackie Kennedy Onassis led the effort to save Grand Central from demolition in the 1970s — the battle resulted in New York City's modern landmarks preservation law.
The terminal has a hidden Oyster Bar restaurant below the main concourse, famous since 1913, and a whispering gallery where you can hear someone speaking softly across the room.

3df06012-4c10-46e1-81d0-55405342e8df.fact: The Upper West Side has been NYC's intellectual and artistic neighborhood since the 1960s — Zabar's deli, the 72nd Street subway, and Riverside Park are the anchors of the local identity.

c173fe45-9067-4582-81ef-cab0aad56afd.fact: Williamsburg's transformation from industrial waterfront to hipster capital was so dramatic it became a sociological phenomenon — the neighborhood is credited with exporting the 'artisanal' aesthetic to cities worldwide.

Before its 2000s reinvention, Williamsburg's waterfront held enormous industrial operations — the Domino Sugar refinery on the East River processed more sugar than anywhere in the world in the early 20th century.

857e40b2-566a-4917-bc08-47920babf3f9.fact: The Van Wyck Expressway serves JFK Airport — it's one of the most consistently congested roads in the city, and taxi drivers have been complaining about it since the airport opened in 1948.
Named for Robert Anderson Van Wyck, New York City's first mayor after consolidation in 1898 — his tenure was notable for corruption scandals that defined machine politics of the era.

5e0c441b-7587-494c-aade-6fea9efc1c73.fact: Harlem became an African-American neighborhood almost by accident — a 1900s real estate crash left buildings empty, and Black families moved in from the overcrowded Tenderloin district downtown.

The Apollo Theater on 125th Street launched careers including James Brown, Ella Fitzgerald, and Michael Jackson — its amateur night, running since 1934, remains one of NYC's most electric live events.

a249b3ee-bde9-4d6d-9a18-9cd2108d2eb0.fact: The Lower East Side was the most densely populated place on Earth around 1900 — over 700 people per acre, as Eastern European Jewish immigrants packed into tenements.

Katz's Delicatessen has been on East Houston since 1888, serving the same pastrami recipe through Prohibition, world wars, and neighborhood transformations — the 'I'll have what she's having' scene from When Harry Met Sally was filmed here.

8c4595b4-b1cd-4707-8fe8-3178c4ca93d1.fact: Fresh Kills Landfill received New York City's garbage for 53 years — at its peak it was the largest man-made structure on Earth by volume, visible from space.
The site is being converted into Freshkills Park, which will eventually be three times the size of Central Park — a remarkable transformation from toxic landfill to ecological restoration project.

c3169415-bb26-4cea-8a12-36afd3e37f18.fact: Northeast Queens — Little Neck, Douglaston, and Bayside — feels more like a Long Island suburb than New York City, with detached houses, quiet streets, and the water of Little Neck Bay visible from many corners.
This part of Queens was largely undeveloped until the LIRR arrived in the 1860s, making it accessible to commuters — the original Victorian train stations are still in use today.

5507fab3-3430-4747-a9ab-ed9f7b5ba959.fact: Queens Boulevard earned the nickname 'the Boulevard of Death' — between 1990 and 1998, 186 pedestrians were killed trying to cross its 12 lanes, spurring a massive safety redesign.
The boulevard runs 7.7 miles from the East River to the Nassau County border, passing through a dozen distinct Queens neighborhoods — essentially a cross-section of the entire borough.

33aa3bec-8143-421c-a0f7-866a2feaa352.fact: F. Scott Fitzgerald wrote of this crossing in The Great Gatsby: 'the city seen from the Queensboro Bridge is always the city seen for the first time, in its first wild promise of all the mystery and beauty in the world.'
Simon & Garfunkel immortalized it as the '59th Street Bridge' in their 1966 hit 'Feelin' Groovy' — locals still call it that.
It's a cantilever bridge, not suspension — the two steel towers support the deck from above rather than hanging it from cables.
Roosevelt Island sits in the middle of the East River beneath the bridge, accessible by a Swiss-made aerial tramway that's been running since 1976.

4f806706-c888-4926-932c-f9f3f0255b60.fact: The Sheridan Expressway and New England Thruway connect the Bronx to I-95 heading north — the key artery for freight and passengers leaving the city toward New England.
This section of the Bronx waterfront was once home to major shipping terminals — today the area is being reconsidered for mixed-use development as freight patterns shift.

418ae598-7cdd-48ff-8aea-97fc3d312407.fact: Atlantic Avenue between Court Street and 4th Avenue has been known as 'Little Syria' since the 1890s — today it's home to Middle Eastern restaurants and bakeries, a culinary time capsule.
Beneath Atlantic Avenue lies an 1844 railroad tunnel — one of the oldest subway tunnels in the world, occasionally opened for tours.

1eb66413-6761-4eb0-bc04-e2533bb74b42.fact: Midtown Manhattan is the engine of the global economy — on any weekday, more business decisions are made per square mile here than almost anywhere on Earth.
The subway system beneath these streets carries more passengers per year than any other rapid transit system in the Western Hemisphere — over a billion rides annually.
Most New Yorkers would rather walk three blocks in a snowstorm than wait for a Midtown cab at rush hour — and they're usually right about the math.

b95471db-03ed-4fc3-9b91-561328029fd4.fact: SoHo's cast-iron building facades are the largest collection of pre-Civil War cast-iron architecture in the world — they were built cheaply to mimic stone but outlasted most actual stone buildings of the era.

Artists occupied SoHo's industrial lofts illegally starting in the 1960s before the city legalized artist-in-residence designation — a loophole that transformed the neighborhood and eventually priced out the artists.

c880d0c4-db84-44c2-9f00-62f21a83b5d0.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

b7e5959c-1fc5-4fd2-a73e-426127b587b1.fact: The Upper West Side has been NYC's intellectual and artistic neighborhood since the 1960s — Zabar's deli, the 72nd Street subway, and Riverside Park are the anchors of the local identity.
Broadway on the Upper West Side retains its diagonal path from the original Bloomingdale Road, creating triangular 'squares' like Verdi Square at 72nd and Straus Park at 106th.

5b44d19e-de48-4941-b071-d2a4c08bd230.fact: Midtown Manhattan is the engine of the global economy — on any weekday, more business decisions are made per square mile here than almost anywhere on Earth.
The subway system beneath these streets carries more passengers per year than any other rapid transit system in the Western Hemisphere — over a billion rides annually.
Most New Yorkers would rather walk three blocks in a snowstorm than wait for a Midtown cab at rush hour — and they're usually right about the math.

5bd248b9-1dab-4118-b4b2-d3ef3fbd7085.fact: Brooklyn Heights was the first NYC neighborhood designated a historic district (1965) — its Federal and Greek Revival brownstones, built by wealthy Manhattan merchants in the 1820s-40s, are some of the finest examples of 19th-century domestic architecture in the US.
The Brooklyn Heights Promenade offers what many consider the best skyline view in New York — the iconic vista of Lower Manhattan across the East River has appeared in countless films and photographs.

b963b25c-9fcf-4a12-b502-c57468510315.fact: F. Scott Fitzgerald wrote of this crossing in The Great Gatsby: 'the city seen from the Queensboro Bridge is always the city seen for the first time, in its first wild promise of all the mystery and beauty in the world.'
Simon & Garfunkel immortalized it as the '59th Street Bridge' in their 1966 hit 'Feelin' Groovy' — locals still call it that.
It's a cantilever bridge, not suspension — the two steel towers support the deck from above rather than hanging it from cables.
Roosevelt Island sits in the middle of the East River beneath the bridge, accessible by a Swiss-made aerial tramway that's been running since 1976.

c6d013c9-e69b-4824-8dad-b6580491d916.fact: Grand Central Terminal opened in 1913 with the world's largest train shed — the main concourse ceiling famously shows a painted constellation map, though it's reversed from the actual sky.
Jackie Kennedy Onassis led the effort to save Grand Central from demolition in the 1970s — the battle resulted in New York City's modern landmarks preservation law.
The terminal has a hidden Oyster Bar restaurant below the main concourse, famous since 1913, and a whispering gallery where you can hear someone speaking softly across the room.

d4add2b5-0a3c-43dd-ad8f-edc72b9ab2e5.fact: The Williamsburg Bridge (1903) was the world's longest suspension bridge for a brief moment after opening — and the first all-steel suspension bridge in the US.
Old-timers called it 'the Jew's Highway' — it connected the teeming Jewish immigrant neighborhoods of the Lower East Side directly to Williamsburg.
Four subway lines (J, M, Z, and formerly the M's older routing) share the bridge deck, making it one of the busiest rail crossings in the city.
Unlike the ornate Brooklyn Bridge, the Williamsburg Bridge has no decorative stonework — just raw steel, which was scandalous to critics in 1903.

5bb6d7d8-39ce-43b8-b539-376fbcf73927.fact: Midtown Manhattan is the engine of the global economy — on any weekday, more business decisions are made per square mile here than almost anywhere on Earth.
The subway system beneath these streets carries more passengers per year than any other rapid transit system in the Western Hemisphere — over a billion rides annually.
Most New Yorkers would rather walk three blocks in a snowstorm than wait for a Midtown cab at rush hour — and they're usually right about the math.

45b87983-6659-4be7-a354-15d756664204.fact: Williamsburg's transformation from industrial waterfront to hipster capital was so dramatic it became a sociological phenomenon — the neighborhood is credited with exporting the 'artisanal' aesthetic to cities worldwide.

Before its 2000s reinvention, Williamsburg's waterfront held enormous industrial operations — the Domino Sugar refinery on the East River processed more sugar than anywhere in the world in the early 20th century.

8ed1baa8-48ab-4ec7-9110-7b89162ce8c3.fact: Hylan Boulevard is named for John Hylan, NYC mayor from 1918-1925, who was famous for fighting the subway companies — he once called the IRT a 'traction ring of piracy.'

Running nearly the full length of Staten Island's east shore, Hylan Blvd is the longest road entirely within Staten Island — and one of the few ways to traverse the borough north to south without the expressway.

9750737e-90f5-4681-9fef-a396f9992540.fact: Williamsburg's transformation from industrial waterfront to hipster capital was so dramatic it became a sociological phenomenon — the neighborhood is credited with exporting the 'artisanal' aesthetic to cities worldwide.

Before its 2000s reinvention, Williamsburg's waterfront held enormous industrial operations — the Domino Sugar refinery on the East River processed more sugar than anywhere in the world in the early 20th century.

c5040f93-f4ec-4803-8370-8b931b0443e2.fact: The FDR Drive runs 9.5 miles along Manhattan's east shore, built largely on landfill created from subway tunnel excavation and Depression-era public works.
Originally called the East River Drive, it was renamed for Franklin D. Roosevelt after his death in 1945 — a fitting tribute to the president who championed public works.
It's technically a parkway, so trucks are banned — the low clearance underpasses were a deliberate design choice to exclude commercial vehicles.

d8122408-7092-41ba-a9db-ef8847edeaef.fact: Times Square was called Longacre Square until 1904, when the New York Times moved its headquarters here and convinced the city to rename it — and add a New Year's Eve ball drop.
At peak times, Times Square hosts 380,000 pedestrians per day — making it one of the most foot-trafficked intersections on the planet.
The famous billboards are technically 'spectaculars' in advertising industry jargon — Times Square has more square footage of signage than almost anywhere on Earth.

3fa07801-69d7-4d21-a807-4156b1b6602a.fact: Brooklyn is technically its own city — the fourth largest in the US by population — that was absorbed into New York City in 1898 against the wishes of many residents who voted against consolidation.

Brooklyn's diversity means you can eat your way around the world within a few subway stops — Haitian in Flatbush, Russian in Brighton Beach, Chinese in Sunset Park, and Dominican in Crown Heights all within an hour.

c6d82dbd-1c49-478f-819f-a38c1826b628.fact: Canal Street marks the northern boundary of Chinatown, one of the densest Chinese-American communities in the Western Hemisphere, with over 100,000 residents in the surrounding blocks.
The street is named for an actual canal that once ran here, draining the Collect Pond — the canal was filled in 1820 and the street was built over it.
Canal Street's sidewalk vendors selling 'designer' watches and handbags are so famous they've become a cultural institution — the city has tried to crack down for decades with limited success.

15f7a00b-5afb-4193-a5e4-dfe7710a5dfe.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

5c2b7782-2fb8-486f-89d2-0c16b6eac70c.fact: The Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883 as the longest suspension bridge on Earth — and the first to use steel wire cables instead of iron.
Chief engineer Washington Roebling directed construction from his bedroom window through binoculars after being crippled by decompression sickness, while his wife Emily supervised on-site.
P.T. Barnum marched 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove it was structurally sound to skeptical New Yorkers — it worked.
About 10,000 pedestrians and cyclists cross the bridge's elevated walkway every day, making it one of NYC's most beloved public spaces.

2e5f0c0e-575d-4323-86ec-63e75acbf129.fact: F. Scott Fitzgerald wrote of this crossing in The Great Gatsby: 'the city seen from the Queensboro Bridge is always the city seen for the first time, in its first wild promise of all the mystery and beauty in the world.'
Simon & Garfunkel immortalized it as the '59th Street Bridge' in their 1966 hit 'Feelin' Groovy' — locals still call it that.
It's a cantilever bridge, not suspension — the two steel towers support the deck from above rather than hanging it from cables.
Roosevelt Island sits in the middle of the East River beneath the bridge, accessible by a Swiss-made aerial tramway that's been running since 1976.

c89b62d1-35be-4bb8-b22b-a77c2583733c.fact: Eastern Parkway, designed by Frederick Law Olmsted in 1870, is considered America's first parkway — predating the term by decades, it established the template for tree-lined boulevard design.
Every Labor Day, Eastern Parkway hosts the West Indian American Day Carnival — one of the largest outdoor events in the US, drawing over a million people to celebrate Caribbean culture.

8385d983-6782-4219-8aab-98f26b224f9d.fact: The original elevated West Side Highway collapsed in 1973 when a cement truck fell through the deck — it was demolished rather than repaired, and the waterfront sat undeveloped for years.
The replacement Route 9A is a ground-level boulevard flanked by the Hudson River Greenway, one of the busiest cycling paths in the United States.

0e8def41-11e2-45de-a8f6-b746b805b7c5.fact: The Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883 as the longest suspension bridge on Earth — and the first to use steel wire cables instead of iron.
Chief engineer Washington Roebling directed construction from his bedroom window through binoculars after being crippled by decompression sickness, while his wife Emily supervised on-site.
P.T. Barnum marched 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove it was structurally sound to skeptical New Yorkers — it worked.
About 10,000 pedestrians and cyclists cross the bridge's elevated walkway every day, making it one of NYC's most beloved public spaces.

14767de3-0b6f-4708-b63a-230f5883d932.fact: The Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883 as the longest suspension bridge on Earth — and the first to use steel wire cables instead of iron.

P.T. Barnum marched 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove it was structurally sound to skeptical New Yorkers — it worked.
About 10,000 pedestrians and cyclists cross the bridge's elevated walkway every day, making it one of NYC's most beloved public spaces.

5c258011-2fa1-4b50-96c9-738ec3cf678c.fact: The Grand Central Parkway was one of Robert Moses's first major projects, opening in 1936 as a showcase of modern highway design — wide lanes, gentle curves, and landscaped margins.
The parkway provides the primary access route to LaGuardia Airport, meaning its traffic level shifts dramatically with flight schedules.
It runs through Cunningham Park and Alley Pond Park, preserving one of Queens's largest stretches of old-growth forest right alongside a busy expressway.

710191bf-5753-49f7-a524-d0db75526600.fact: The St. George neighborhood at Staten Island's northern tip is undergoing a revival — the ferry terminal, a minor league baseball stadium, and the Snug Harbor Cultural Center are drawing visitors who have never explored the borough before.

The Staten Island Ferry is free and has been since 1997 — 70,000 riders a day make the crossing for work or pleasure, with one of the best views of the Statue of Liberty and Lower Manhattan skyline of any transit ride in the world.

c90f122a-2ea4-4614-b434-3599bcb04a19.fact: The Hutchinson River Parkway is named for Anne Hutchinson, the religious dissenter who settled the Bronx-Westchester area in 1642 after being banished from Massachusetts Bay Colony.
The parkway follows a tidal estuary through Pelham Bay Park — one of the few places in the city where you can watch herons fishing from the highway median.

bcafb919-3f51-4caa-b964-d6888ed4cecf.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.
The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

9eca011a-81b1-4e4a-b44a-4ee2bc8affff.fact: The East Village was the center of punk rock in the 1970s (CBGB was on the Bowery), the countercultural movement in the 1960s, and a major Ukrainian immigrant community going back to the 1800s — all at once.
The neighborhood's St. Marks Place has been famous for cheap, diverse food since the 1970s — Japanese curry, Ukrainian pierogies, and NYC pizza slices still coexist within a few blocks.

d892897d-5a64-4450-9502-2e91b3ce5eea.fact: F. Scott Fitzgerald wrote of this crossing in The Great Gatsby: 'the city seen from the Queensboro Bridge is always the city seen for the first time, in its first wild promise of all the mystery and beauty in the world.'
Simon & Garfunkel immortalized it as the '59th Street Bridge' in their 1966 hit 'Feelin' Groovy' — locals still call it that.
It's a cantilever bridge, not suspension — the two steel towers support the deck from above rather than hanging it from cables.
Roosevelt Island sits in the middle of the East River beneath the bridge, accessible by a Swiss-made aerial tramway that's been running since 1976.

64ed1f5d-ba90-4c12-afda-9a9c3e658efd.fact: Ocean Parkway (1876) was designed by Olmsted and Vaux as a scenic carriage road connecting Prospect Park to Coney Island — it's a National Scenic Byway and one of the oldest parkways in the US.
The central pedestrian and cycling mall running the length of Ocean Parkway has been used for leisure for nearly 150 years — one of Brooklyn's most pleasant walks.

5e0a8966-79ae-4a7b-9561-aca2c37b22ff.fact: The LIE earned its nickname 'the world's longest parking lot' so thoroughly that traffic reporters have their own lore about which stretch is moving today.
Construction began in 1937 and continued piecemeal for three decades as Queens suburbanized — you can see the highway's character shift from urban to suburban as you go east.
At peak hours, the LIE carries over 200,000 vehicles per day through Queens — one of the most congested stretches of highway in the United States.

67a7ce68-80ad-46d3-930c-7cf200109be7.fact: The Staten Island Expressway (I-278) is the main east-west artery of the island — it was supposed to connect to an outer belt highway that was never built, leaving it as a stub.
Construction in the 1950s and 60s required demolishing hundreds of homes in Sunnyside and other mid-island neighborhoods, following the same Moses playbook used in Brooklyn.

c8d37f98-5b6f-4a93-bfc5-eadf4f91726a.fact: The Rockaways peninsula is NYC's most unusual geography — a long barrier beach accessible by subway, where city residents surf, lifeguard, and maintain beach bungalows within the five boroughs.
The Rockaways were a major resort destination in the early 1900s, packed with hotels and bathhouses — Hurricane Sandy in 2012 devastated the community, spurring a remarkable rebuilding effort.

500b7258-e2ec-4717-8f99-a5497e158ae6.fact: The Outerbridge Crossing is named for Eugenius Harvey Outerbridge, the first chairman of the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey — not because it's the outermost bridge, though it is.
Opened in 1928, it's a continuous truss bridge spanning the Arthur Kill — its counterpart, the Goethals Bridge, opened the same day just to the north.

c914c9ae-00b2-4b77-abb9-0a85f903e34e.fact: This part of the northeast Bronx was developed in the early 20th century as middle-class housing — brick apartment buildings and two-family homes replaced farmland as subway lines extended north.

Nearby Bronx Park is home to both the Bronx Zoo and the New York Botanical Garden — together they make this corner of the borough one of the greatest concentrations of wildlife and plant life in any city on Earth.

6ab24015-2446-4410-b7a4-d8dd13ed2f8b.fact: The LIE earned its nickname 'the world's longest parking lot' so thoroughly that traffic reporters have their own lore about which stretch is moving today.
Construction began in 1937 and continued piecemeal for three decades as Queens suburbanized — you can see the highway's character shift from urban to suburban as you go east.
At peak hours, the LIE carries over 200,000 vehicles per day through Queens — one of the most congested stretches of highway in the United States.

0952329e-6b6f-4286-b5e3-b682eae94e52.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

426d2789-3f58-4c5b-9db6-1647db7c4b5a.fact: The Belt Parkway was designed in the 1930s as a scenic shore drive — Robert Moses imagined New Yorkers leisurely cruising along the coastline on Sunday afternoons.
The low underpasses were deliberately built too low for buses, allegedly to keep poor New Yorkers (who relied on public transit) from accessing the waterfront parks.
The parkway runs along Jamaica Bay, offering glimpses of one of the largest urban wildlife refuges in the US — home to migratory shorebirds and harbor seals.

5352e130-4668-4be5-a7b9-9e1ce4ea6d4c.fact: Midtown Manhattan is the engine of the global economy — on any weekday, more business decisions are made per square mile here than almost anywhere on Earth.

Most New Yorkers would rather walk three blocks in a snowstorm than wait for a Midtown cab at rush hour — and they're usually right about the math.

84a2c36a-ef84-41b6-a4eb-3c3a2bf3bf10.fact: Coney Island was America's most famous amusement destination from the 1880s to the 1950s — at its peak, three competing parks (Luna Park, Dreamland, and Steeplechase) hosted millions of visitors each summer.
Nathan's Famous hot dog stand opened on Stillwell Avenue in 1916 and pioneered what we now call fast food — the annual hot dog eating contest on July 4th has been drawing crowds since 1916.

0360205b-183b-4dd3-a01f-e7f0deb5ff1f.fact: The Rockaways peninsula is NYC's most unusual geography — a long barrier beach accessible by subway, where city residents surf, lifeguard, and maintain beach bungalows within the five boroughs.

At peak hours, the LIE carries over 200,000 vehicles per day through Queens — one of the most congested stretches of highway in the United States.

c941aa1a-ebde-4589-9669-eaf67279dfab.fact: Fresh Kills Landfill received New York City's garbage for 53 years — at its peak it was the largest man-made structure on Earth by volume, visible from space.
The site is being converted into Freshkills Park, which will eventually be three times the size of Central Park — a remarkable transformation from toxic landfill to ecological restoration project.

c1b36d36-ce3d-43e3-af83-0cb29bd67e10.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

36d22d6d-bffd-4466-8a9c-9c78a1bb9021.fact: Victory Boulevard was named to commemorate the Allied victory in World War I — Staten Island's main east-west artery bears a name chosen in the patriotic fervor of 1919.
The boulevard passes through Staten Island's most densely developed central corridor, connecting the St. George ferry terminal area to the western shore.

54e7c710-bdef-411d-801a-806060a418f6.fact: Father Capodanno Boulevard is named for Vincent Capodanno, a Staten Island-born Navy chaplain killed in Vietnam in 1967 while protecting wounded Marines — he was posthumously awarded the Medal of Honor.
The boulevard runs along the Staten Island beachfront at South Beach, one of the borough's few public beaches — local families have been coming here since the 1920s.

444aa32f-8102-4ca6-a7f8-5c9fd8fd5571.fact: SoHo's cast-iron building facades are the largest collection of pre-Civil War cast-iron architecture in the world — they were built cheaply to mimic stone but outlasted most actual stone buildings of the era.

ca545979-d0c1-4735-88d6-61b6311bb6bc.fact: Named for English explorer Henry Hudson, who sailed up the river in 1609 aboard the Half Moon while searching for a northwest passage to Asia — and found Manhattan instead.
The parkway runs alongside the Palisades cliffs, some of the most dramatic natural scenery in the metro area, rising 300 feet above the Hudson River opposite.
The Henry Hudson Bridge at its northern end was the first major toll bridge built by the Triborough Bridge Authority — and Robert Moses personally chose the crossing location.

77794e28-5ee9-4fdd-8c14-2615c46d8a40.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.

The parkway runs along Jamaica Bay, offering glimpses of one of the largest urban wildlife refuges in the US — home to migratory shorebirds and harbor seals.

c217a64e-95c3-4442-96d1-7a6c177615d7.fact: Central Park West is home to the Dakota, where John Lennon lived and was shot in 1980 — fans still gather at Strawberry Fields in the park directly across the street.
The street is lined with some of NYC's grandest apartment buildings, including the San Remo, Beresford, and Majestic — all built in the 1920s and 30s for the city's elite.

5ec4ce2f-d86d-4899-b464-436fdcde72eb.fact: The Staten Island Expressway (I-278) is the main east-west artery of the island — it was supposed to connect to an outer belt highway that was never built, leaving it as a stub.

0537e9a6-3e6f-480c-9c6e-bb3ad15356ae.fact: Richmond Avenue on Staten Island feels more like suburban New Jersey than New York City — strip malls, parking lots, and car dealerships dominate what is technically part of the five boroughs.
The Staten Island Mall nearby is one of the largest malls in New York State — and a reminder that Staten Island has the highest car ownership rate of any NYC borough.

81c2390a-2dc5-4a44-9fc3-9870b360032e.fact: Jamaica was a major colonial settlement long before it became part of New York City — it was the seat of Queens County government and a bustling market town in the 1700s.
Jamaica is one of the most important transit hubs in Queens — the E, J, and Z trains meet here, along with the Long Island Rail Road's main line and the AirTrain to JFK.

7e13d5e6-bd1a-47af-ba28-39c50e2736c2.fact: The area near Jerome Avenue and Sedgwick Avenue in the Bronx is where hip-hop culture was born — DJ Kool Herc's historic 1973 party at 1520 Sedgwick Avenue launched a global movement.
The Yankee Stadium neighborhood has seen enormous reinvestment since the new stadium opened in 2009 — but locals still debate whether the gleaming new complex replaced the beloved original.

15fca74d-c3e9-42ee-8f32-5b708ef36477.fact: The Hutchinson River Parkway is named for Anne Hutchinson, the religious dissenter who settled the Bronx-Westchester area in 1642 after being banished from Massachusetts Bay Colony.
The parkway follows a tidal estuary through Pelham Bay Park — one of the few places in the city where you can watch herons fishing from the highway median.

cbb04872-5f62-4989-acaa-e4166fa7c819.fact: Park Slope's brownstones were built in the 1880s-1900s for Brooklyn's upper-middle class and fell into disrepair by the 1960s — their revival through gentrification became the model for urban neighborhood transformation nationwide.
Prospect Park, designed by Olmsted and Vaux as their own improvement on Central Park, anchors the neighborhood — they considered it their finest achievement.

60793334-580b-4538-b947-7312e95039bf.fact: Astoria was the heart of Greek-American NYC for decades — it still has Greek bakeries, coffee shops, and social clubs, though it's now home to a dozen other immigrant communities as well.
Paramount Pictures had its main studio in Astoria in the 1920s and 30s — the facility survived and is now the Kaufman Astoria Studios, where Sesame Street and dozens of films are still produced.

dd374f35-08c9-482d-ae36-ce8e7c0cfb54.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.
The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

99bd1846-c0e4-4689-b077-63d2525bf1aa.fact: Washington Heights above 181st Street is the cultural capital of the Dominican diaspora — more Dominicans live here than in any US city outside the Dominican Republic itself.
The neighborhood's bodegas, Dominican restaurants, and roadside food carts serve some of NYC's best mangu, chicharrones de pollo, and café con leche — try anywhere with a crowd outside.

1ccb8d7c-43d4-450e-b40c-79527766db75.fact: Washington Heights above 181st Street is the cultural capital of the Dominican diaspora — more Dominicans live here than in any US city outside the Dominican Republic itself.

3f04a686-f97c-4187-8968-cb09265e08ff.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

cf6d1124-f012-4678-9277-2dca8d18232b.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.
The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

62380b5a-8960-4c2b-9125-d7f088605a7f.fact: The East Village was the center of punk rock in the 1970s (CBGB was on the Bowery), the countercultural movement in the 1960s, and a major Ukrainian immigrant community going back to the 1800s — all at once.
The neighborhood's St. Marks Place has been famous for cheap, diverse food since the 1970s — Japanese curry, Ukrainian pierogies, and NYC pizza slices still coexist within a few blocks.

6504ada3-3a93-4811-b2cd-31c377066f66.fact: The LIE earned its nickname 'the world's longest parking lot' so thoroughly that traffic reporters have their own lore about which stretch is moving today.
Construction began in 1937 and continued piecemeal for three decades as Queens suburbanized — you can see the highway's character shift from urban to suburban as you go east.
At peak hours, the LIE carries over 200,000 vehicles per day through Queens — one of the most congested stretches of highway in the United States.

d019c28c-80f5-490b-8a35-198477ac3fb0.fact: Woodhaven Boulevard was carved through Queens in the 1890s following the old Jamaica Plank Road — a tollroad that connected Jamaica to the Brooklyn ferry before bridges existed.
The boulevard skirts the edge of Forest Park, one of Queens's largest green spaces — 538 acres of kettle ponds, glacial ridges, and oaks that have stood for over 200 years.

83d034f2-584b-4dab-9e3d-d70a59995dc5.fact: The Grand Concourse was laid out in 1909, inspired by the Champs-Élysées — a 180-foot-wide boulevard designed to give the Bronx its own monumental civic spine.
The concourse is lined with some of New York's finest Art Deco apartment buildings, built in the 1920s and 30s for the Bronx's aspirational middle class — a designated historic district.
Yankee Stadium anchors the south end of the Concourse — on game days, the boulevard transforms into a procession of fans heading north from the 161st Street subway stop.

5531396b-856d-429b-8bde-a845ef87aead.fact: Named for English explorer Henry Hudson, who sailed up the river in 1609 aboard the Half Moon while searching for a northwest passage to Asia — and found Manhattan instead.
The parkway runs alongside the Palisades cliffs, some of the most dramatic natural scenery in the metro area, rising 300 feet above the Hudson River opposite.
The Henry Hudson Bridge at its northern end was the first major toll bridge built by the Triborough Bridge Authority — and Robert Moses personally chose the crossing location.

cbdd2872-4e00-4699-9679-871bc2c30834.fact: Brooklyn Heights was the first NYC neighborhood designated a historic district (1965) — its Federal and Greek Revival brownstones, built by wealthy Manhattan merchants in the 1820s-40s, are some of the finest examples of 19th-century domestic architecture in the US.
The Brooklyn Heights Promenade offers what many consider the best skyline view in New York — the iconic vista of Lower Manhattan across the East River has appeared in countless films and photographs.

cd229284-a743-4ac4-9430-366f11e68d6c.fact: Union Square has been the city's protest ground since the 1800s — suffragettes, labor unions, anti-war demonstrators, and Occupy Wall Street have all gathered here.
The Union Square Greenmarket (open Mon, Wed, Fri, Sat) is the city's most famous farmers market, drawing chefs from across Manhattan every week since 1976.

0f3b6031-fe36-43df-b2c7-6120e0580309.fact: The Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883 as the longest suspension bridge on Earth — and the first to use steel wire cables instead of iron.
Chief engineer Washington Roebling directed construction from his bedroom window through binoculars after being crippled by decompression sickness, while his wife Emily supervised on-site.
P.T. Barnum marched 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove it was structurally sound to skeptical New Yorkers — it worked.
About 10,000 pedestrians and cyclists cross the bridge's elevated walkway every day, making it one of NYC's most beloved public spaces.

8933100a-8229-42fe-b4b2-265db410d7ae.fact: Midtown Manhattan is the engine of the global economy — on any weekday, more business decisions are made per square mile here than almost anywhere on Earth.
The subway system beneath these streets carries more passengers per year than any other rapid transit system in the Western Hemisphere — over a billion rides annually.
Most New Yorkers would rather walk three blocks in a snowstorm than wait for a Midtown cab at rush hour — and they're usually right about the math.

cde75072-a814-46b4-8785-765b314c43e8.fact: The West Shore Expressway runs alongside the Arthur Kill waterway separating Staten Island from New Jersey — a tidal strait so industrialized it was once called 'the most polluted body of water in the world.'
The land along the West Shore was home to oyster beds that supplied Manhattan restaurants until industrial pollution killed the beds in the early 20th century.

It's technically a parkway, so trucks are banned — the low clearance underpasses were a deliberate design choice to exclude commercial vehicles.

9e3164a8-1c63-4790-bc24-f9d752f3ae41.fact: The Belt Parkway was designed in the 1930s as a scenic shore drive — Robert Moses imagined New Yorkers leisurely cruising along the coastline on Sunday afternoons.
The low underpasses were deliberately built too low for buses, allegedly to keep poor New Yorkers (who relied on public transit) from accessing the waterfront parks.
The parkway runs along Jamaica Bay, offering glimpses of one of the largest urban wildlife refuges in the US — home to migratory shorebirds and harbor seals.

a8805cdb-e305-42d0-b011-cb825380cf8e.fact: Bushwick is home to one of NYC's largest outdoor art projects — hundreds of massive murals cover buildings throughout the neighborhood, drawing tourists and inspiring street artists from around the world.
Bushwick was home to over 40 breweries in the 1880s, earning it the nickname 'the Brewers' Town' — German immigrants built an entire beer-making industry here before Prohibition killed it off.

2064d8ab-597a-45d8-a3c9-5ab95a4e69e6.fact: Park Slope's brownstones were built in the 1880s-1900s for Brooklyn's upper-middle class and fell into disrepair by the 1960s — their revival through gentrification became the model for urban neighborhood transformation nationwide.
Prospect Park, designed by Olmsted and Vaux as their own improvement on Central Park, anchors the neighborhood — they considered it their finest achievement.

074b4e06-5090-47a5-b672-8b15780c9255.fact: The Upper West Side has been NYC's intellectual and artistic neighborhood since the 1960s — Zabar's deli, the 72nd Street subway, and Riverside Park are the anchors of the local identity.
Broadway on the Upper West Side retains its diagonal path from the original Bloomingdale Road, creating triangular 'squares' like Verdi Square at 72nd and Straus Park at 106th.

The neighborhood's St. Marks Place has been famous for cheap, diverse food since the 1970s — Japanese curry, Ukrainian pierogies, and NYC pizza slices still coexist within a few blocks.

9d7c4525-fba3-4ffe-ae79-f814f7dccf4e.fact: Jackson Heights is one of the most linguistically diverse neighborhoods on Earth — residents speak over 160 languages, earning it the nickname 'the most diverse square mile on the planet.'
Roosevelt Avenue and 74th Street in Jackson Heights is the center of South Asian NYC — Little India's restaurants, sari shops, and sweet shops make it the best place in the city for biryani.

ddd3f06a-7e0e-4e00-b76a-52a511e9009d.fact: The Van Wyck Expressway serves JFK Airport — it's one of the most consistently congested roads in the city, and taxi drivers have been complaining about it since the airport opened in 1948.
Named for Robert Anderson Van Wyck, New York City's first mayor after consolidation in 1898 — his tenure was notable for corruption scandals that defined machine politics of the era.

d138b651-e2ed-4065-a0f9-ef56a65f61d4.fact: TriBeCa (Triangle Below Canal) was a wholesale warehouse district until the 1970s, when artists priced out of SoHo moved into its cast-iron lofts — Robert De Niro then helped transform it into one of NYC's priciest neighborhoods.
The TriBeCa Film Festival, founded by Robert De Niro after 9/11, helped revitalize lower Manhattan and has screened thousands of independent films since 2002.

d016d3ce-4399-4487-a1a8-2c16620a251d.fact: Canarsie Pier juts into Jamaica Bay and was once the terminal of the Brooklyn-Manhattan Transit's Canarsie Line — now the L train terminus, the old elevated rail structure was demolished in the 1950s.
Canarsie borders the Jamaica Bay Wildlife Refuge, where over 330 bird species have been recorded — a remarkable sanctuary within city limits, accessible by kayak from the pier.

d1e071f1-57c6-4e6a-b813-0c50b90f7b1a.fact: Roosevelt Avenue under the 7 train is one of NYC's greatest food streets — Colombian, Ecuadorian, Nepalese, Mexican, and dozens of other cuisines compete for space beneath the elevated tracks.
The 7 train above Roosevelt Avenue is sometimes called 'the International Express' — it passes through more immigrant communities per mile than almost any transit line in the world.

b9c20141-52ca-43a1-a7df-c2577ca384b6.fact: Queens is the most ethnically diverse urban area on Earth — home to immigrants from over 100 countries who have created a patchwork of distinct communities across the borough.
Queens has a unique geography — it's where NYC's suburban character meets its urban density, with detached houses on quiet streets just miles from the world's busiest transit hub.

bdf81be6-bce0-4dfc-91d4-946647f2553e.fact: Woodhaven Boulevard was carved through Queens in the 1890s following the old Jamaica Plank Road — a tollroad that connected Jamaica to the Brooklyn ferry before bridges existed.
The boulevard skirts the edge of Forest Park, one of Queens's largest green spaces — 538 acres of kettle ponds, glacial ridges, and oaks that have stood for over 200 years.

d67e45e9-034f-4bd9-9bc3-12d07a12af48.fact: Harlem became an African-American neighborhood almost by accident — a 1900s real estate crash left buildings empty, and Black families moved in from the overcrowded Tenderloin district downtown.

The Apollo Theater on 125th Street launched careers including James Brown, Ella Fitzgerald, and Michael Jackson — its amateur night, running since 1934, remains one of NYC's most electric live events.

e08c24be-e2fe-4d83-bc08-bf88cdebdd63.fact: Washington Heights above 181st Street is the cultural capital of the Dominican diaspora — more Dominicans live here than in any US city outside the Dominican Republic itself.
The neighborhood's bodegas, Dominican restaurants, and roadside food carts serve some of NYC's best mangu, chicharrones de pollo, and café con leche — try anywhere with a crowd outside.

d7496b4f-f3b5-4a49-89d3-4212e0606205.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.

b995be5a-a469-4cf6-b2ee-197e8e9eecd9.fact: Howard Beach is one of the few NYC neighborhoods with almost no subway access — residents depend heavily on the A train's JFK branch or the AirTrain, giving it an unusually suburban feel.

Cross Bay Boulevard here crosses Jamaica Bay itself, giving drivers a brief water-level view of the bay and the wildlife refuge — a genuinely unexpected moment of natural beauty in the outer boroughs.

e51a2974-8ca2-4fe2-a90a-842685cbbcc9.fact: Robert Moses displaced an estimated 60,000 Bronx residents to build the Cross Bronx Expressway in the 1950s — described by urban historian Robert Caro as one of the most destructive acts of urban planning in American history.
The communities bulldozed and isolated by the Cross Bronx gave birth to hip-hop in the 1970s — young people in its shadow invented a new culture from the wreckage.
As part of I-95, the Cross Bronx is one of the most critical freight corridors in the Northeast — and one of the most congested stretches of interstate in the US.

cd52aa01-1d36-4d0a-bf03-79cb78ffadae.fact: Brooklyn Heights was the first NYC neighborhood designated a historic district (1965) — its Federal and Greek Revival brownstones, built by wealthy Manhattan merchants in the 1820s-40s, are some of the finest examples of 19th-century domestic architecture in the US.
The Brooklyn Heights Promenade offers what many consider the best skyline view in New York — the iconic vista of Lower Manhattan across the East River has appeared in countless films and photographs.

The low underpasses were deliberately built too low for buses, allegedly to keep poor New Yorkers (who relied on public transit) from accessing the waterfront parks.
The parkway runs along Jamaica Bay, offering glimpses of one of the largest urban wildlife refuges in the US — home to migratory shorebirds and harbor seals.

b47d139f-69ae-427e-86b4-f970f9e990e1.fact: Flushing's Main Street is the center of one of the largest Chinatowns in the US outside of Manhattan — you can find regional Chinese cuisines here that you can't find anywhere else in New York.
Flushing Meadows-Corona Park hosted two World's Fairs (1939 and 1964) — the Unisphere, the 140-foot steel globe built for the 1964 fair, still stands as one of NYC's most distinctive landmarks.

e5cd3feb-ca2f-4108-b6ae-678b5ec32dab.fact: The Bronx's Concourse area near 161st Street is anchored by Yankee Stadium — the new 2009 stadium sits across the street from where the original 'House That Ruth Built' stood from 1923 to 2008.
Grand Concourse near 161st is the symbolic center of Bronx civic life — the Bronx County Courthouse, a stunning Art Deco building, presides over the neighborhood.

d05b98ae-f2a7-4c26-8238-754081877484.fact: Northern Boulevard is one of the most ethnically diverse commercial strips in the world — within a few miles you'll pass Korean, Bangladeshi, Colombian, Indian, and Greek business districts.
The boulevard follows the old Jamaica Road, the colonial-era route connecting Jamaica (the Queens town, not the island) to the western ferry landings.

e278de5c-4cfa-46e7-aa85-59512db61f0d.fact: The FDR Drive runs 9.5 miles along Manhattan's east shore, built largely on landfill created from subway tunnel excavation and Depression-era public works.
Originally called the East River Drive, it was renamed for Franklin D. Roosevelt after his death in 1945 — a fitting tribute to the president who championed public works.
It's technically a parkway, so trucks are banned — the low clearance underpasses were a deliberate design choice to exclude commercial vehicles.

6d4bed01-9b6f-45c1-ae3a-159ca91125ba.fact: F. Scott Fitzgerald wrote of this crossing in The Great Gatsby: 'the city seen from the Queensboro Bridge is always the city seen for the first time, in its first wild promise of all the mystery and beauty in the world.'
Simon & Garfunkel immortalized it as the '59th Street Bridge' in their 1966 hit 'Feelin' Groovy' — locals still call it that.
It's a cantilever bridge, not suspension — the two steel towers support the deck from above rather than hanging it from cables.
Roosevelt Island sits in the middle of the East River beneath the bridge, accessible by a Swiss-made aerial tramway that's been running since 1976.

ad00e0d3-02f2-4578-b0f1-b9d176d117d1.fact: Richmond Avenue on Staten Island feels more like suburban New Jersey than New York City — strip malls, parking lots, and car dealerships dominate what is technically part of the five boroughs.
The Staten Island Mall nearby is one of the largest malls in New York State — and a reminder that Staten Island has the highest car ownership rate of any NYC borough.

1546f761-039c-4b5c-af5e-75c83c9f603f.fact: Grand Central Terminal opened in 1913 with the world's largest train shed — the main concourse ceiling famously shows a painted constellation map, though it's reversed from the actual sky.
Jackie Kennedy Onassis led the effort to save Grand Central from demolition in the 1970s — the battle resulted in New York City's modern landmarks preservation law.
The terminal has a hidden Oyster Bar restaurant below the main concourse, famous since 1913, and a whispering gallery where you can hear someone speaking softly across the room.

1f81dbf0-4360-49fd-b768-b1a09d730eb1.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.
The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

d2940392-4ee6-433a-ae24-f4f4f15f2566.fact: The Manhattan Bridge carries the B, D, N, and Q subway lines — more train service than any other East River crossing.
Its anchorage entrance features a colonnade of Corinthian columns and triumphal arches, a surprisingly grand approach for what is essentially a commuter bridge.
Opened in 1909, the bridge's cables had to be completely replaced in the 1990s — the original design underestimated the weight of modern subway trains.
The bridge appears in the opening shot of Woody Allen's Manhattan (1979), framed against the sunrise — one of NYC's most iconic cinematic images.

2bc8aef2-fc97-47e0-84cd-1efc8ee21bfe.fact: The East Village was the center of punk rock in the 1970s (CBGB was on the Bowery), the countercultural movement in the 1960s, and a major Ukrainian immigrant community going back to the 1800s — all at once.
The neighborhood's St. Marks Place has been famous for cheap, diverse food since the 1970s — Japanese curry, Ukrainian pierogies, and NYC pizza slices still coexist within a few blocks.

b2924000-6eb1-4449-b201-01c0653b6c68.fact: Pelham Parkway borders Pelham Bay Park, the largest park in New York City — three times the size of Central Park, with salt marshes, forests, and 13 miles of shoreline.
The parkway was designed by Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux (the Central Park team) as a grand tree-lined boulevard connecting the park to the city.

d7d6c994-492d-4f3a-895e-745596825fb4.fact: The area near Jerome Avenue and Sedgwick Avenue in the Bronx is where hip-hop culture was born — DJ Kool Herc's historic 1973 party at 1520 Sedgwick Avenue launched a global movement.
The Yankee Stadium neighborhood has seen enormous reinvestment since the new stadium opened in 2009 — but locals still debate whether the gleaming new complex replaced the beloved original.

f2b94b32-fc41-42b6-935d-8330374ca05a.fact: Harlem became an African-American neighborhood almost by accident — a 1900s real estate crash left buildings empty, and Black families moved in from the overcrowded Tenderloin district downtown.

The Apollo Theater on 125th Street launched careers including James Brown, Ella Fitzgerald, and Michael Jackson — its amateur night, running since 1934, remains one of NYC's most electric live events.

ef24beea-023c-4377-86c9-f9c8836c961b.fact: The Staten Island Expressway (I-278) is the main east-west artery of the island — it was supposed to connect to an outer belt highway that was never built, leaving it as a stub.
Construction in the 1950s and 60s required demolishing hundreds of homes in Sunnyside and other mid-island neighborhoods, following the same Moses playbook used in Brooklyn.

695c4b6d-78d1-4478-81e2-55c8e1acea68.fact: The Staten Island Expressway (I-278) is the main east-west artery of the island — it was supposed to connect to an outer belt highway that was never built, leaving it as a stub.
Construction in the 1950s and 60s required demolishing hundreds of homes in Sunnyside and other mid-island neighborhoods, following the same Moses playbook used in Brooklyn.

5361a6ea-95fe-44e1-99f8-dc7d6964a427.fact: Flatbush Avenue follows the path of an ancient Lenape trail, later the main road through the Dutch colonial town of Flatbush — one of Brooklyn's original six Dutch settlements.
The avenue runs 11 miles from the Manhattan Bridge to Floyd Bennett Field at Jamaica Bay, cutting diagonally across Brooklyn's grid in a way that creates dozens of awkward intersections.

7d10cfbb-0465-4958-90fc-ddd5349d6635.fact: Canal Street marks the northern boundary of Chinatown, one of the densest Chinese-American communities in the Western Hemisphere, with over 100,000 residents in the surrounding blocks.
The street is named for an actual canal that once ran here, draining the Collect Pond — the canal was filled in 1820 and the street was built over it.
Canal Street's sidewalk vendors selling 'designer' watches and handbags are so famous they've become a cultural institution — the city has tried to crack down for decades with limited success.

5aa32e02-77f7-436b-9887-fbb7a84a3489.fact: Northeast Queens — Little Neck, Douglaston, and Bayside — feels more like a Long Island suburb than New York City, with detached houses, quiet streets, and the water of Little Neck Bay visible from many corners.
This part of Queens was largely undeveloped until the LIRR arrived in the 1860s, making it accessible to commuters — the original Victorian train stations are still in use today.

db1570e7-17a4-4916-9f42-cc8ca17aa42e.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

d83aed40-b117-424b-8caf-35c3afe82527.fact: F. Scott Fitzgerald wrote of this crossing in The Great Gatsby: 'the city seen from the Queensboro Bridge is always the city seen for the first time, in its first wild promise of all the mystery and beauty in the world.'
Simon & Garfunkel immortalized it as the '59th Street Bridge' in their 1966 hit 'Feelin' Groovy' — locals still call it that.
It's a cantilever bridge, not suspension — the two steel towers support the deck from above rather than hanging it from cables.
Roosevelt Island sits in the middle of the East River beneath the bridge, accessible by a Swiss-made aerial tramway that's been running since 1976.

efa9a3d5-e5fa-440e-8f6e-68436b8c397b.fact: Harlem became an African-American neighborhood almost by accident — a 1900s real estate crash left buildings empty, and Black families moved in from the overcrowded Tenderloin district downtown.

34616ac5-0cc5-4b86-ac7a-82c77238a536.fact: Lower Manhattan was the entirety of New York City in 1660 — the Dutch colonial town of New Amsterdam fit within the modern blocks between Battery Park and Wall Street.
The Financial District's street grid follows the old Dutch colonial paths, which is why Broadway curves and streets like Beaver and Stone have colonial-era names.

5c29ba71-1757-45db-8318-e1a2bad57e75.fact: Queens Boulevard earned the nickname 'the Boulevard of Death' — between 1990 and 1998, 186 pedestrians were killed trying to cross its 12 lanes, spurring a massive safety redesign.
The boulevard runs 7.7 miles from the East River to the Nassau County border, passing through a dozen distinct Queens neighborhoods — essentially a cross-section of the entire borough.

d9cd00ff-9b8e-492f-8b02-db27b6cf847c.fact: The Brooklyn Bridge opened in 1883 as the longest suspension bridge on Earth — and the first to use steel wire cables instead of iron.
Chief engineer Washington Roebling directed construction from his bedroom window through binoculars after being crippled by decompression sickness, while his wife Emily supervised on-site.
P.T. Barnum marched 21 elephants across in 1884 to prove it was structurally sound to skeptical New Yorkers — it worked.
About 10,000 pedestrians and cyclists cross the bridge's elevated walkway every day, making it one of NYC's most beloved public spaces.

d872e1a0-8c5f-47da-9469-bf40a3673ade.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.
The famous triple-cantilever section in Brooklyn Heights has a public promenade on its roof, giving residents Manhattan skyline views in exchange for decades of noise and fumes.
The BQE is slowly falling apart — engineers are debating a $4 billion reconstruction that would take years and might demolish the beloved Brooklyn Heights Promenade.

d88837a0-5f2d-4800-b4c8-57970aa929cd.fact: The Rockaways peninsula is NYC's most unusual geography — a long barrier beach accessible by subway, where city residents surf, lifeguard, and maintain beach bungalows within the five boroughs.
The Rockaways were a major resort destination in the early 1900s, packed with hotels and bathhouses — Hurricane Sandy in 2012 devastated the community, spurring a remarkable rebuilding effort.

9c0718c5-0ae9-4170-bea0-9b5d7d61e0c4.fact: The Williamsburg Bridge (1903) was the world's longest suspension bridge for a brief moment after opening — and the first all-steel suspension bridge in the US.
Old-timers called it 'the Jew's Highway' — it connected the teeming Jewish immigrant neighborhoods of the Lower East Side directly to Williamsburg.
Four subway lines (J, M, Z, and formerly the M's older routing) share the bridge deck, making it one of the busiest rail crossings in the city.
Unlike the ornate Brooklyn Bridge, the Williamsburg Bridge has no decorative stonework — just raw steel, which was scandalous to critics in 1903.

4c3de40f-6f13-491f-9253-1fc93cada807.fact: Richmond Avenue on Staten Island feels more like suburban New Jersey than New York City — strip malls, parking lots, and car dealerships dominate what is technically part of the five boroughs.
The Staten Island Mall nearby is one of the largest malls in New York State — and a reminder that Staten Island has the highest car ownership rate of any NYC borough.

db028e65-b4b4-4f83-a427-6be6cb3f2ace.fact: Washington Heights above 181st Street is the cultural capital of the Dominican diaspora — more Dominicans live here than in any US city outside the Dominican Republic itself.
The neighborhood's bodegas, Dominican restaurants, and roadside food carts serve some of NYC's best mangu, chicharrones de pollo, and café con leche — try anywhere with a crowd outside.

69c109a1-a8dd-4164-bbc1-88497dc67c1e.fact: The Manhattan Bridge carries the B, D, N, and Q subway lines — more train service than any other East River crossing.

The bridge appears in the opening shot of Woody Allen's Manhattan (1979), framed against the sunrise — one of NYC's most iconic cinematic images.

f419b38e-2b68-4659-a525-d0a31add8528.fact: Park Slope's brownstones were built in the 1880s-1900s for Brooklyn's upper-middle class and fell into disrepair by the 1960s — their revival through gentrification became the model for urban neighborhood transformation nationwide.
Prospect Park, designed by Olmsted and Vaux as their own improvement on Central Park, anchors the neighborhood — they considered it their finest achievement.

6fc602cf-72c5-40cc-9a42-9a844de7bc1a.fact: The Lower East Side was the most densely populated place on Earth around 1900 — over 700 people per acre, as Eastern European Jewish immigrants packed into tenements.

Katz's Delicatessen has been on East Houston since 1888, serving the same pastrami recipe through Prohibition, world wars, and neighborhood transformations — the 'I'll have what she's having' scene from When Harry Met Sally was filmed here.

dfed3e07-de07-4243-8a41-509a1800cd1e.fact: Brooklyn is technically its own city — the fourth largest in the US by population — that was absorbed into New York City in 1898 against the wishes of many residents who voted against consolidation.

Brooklyn's diversity means you can eat your way around the world within a few subway stops — Haitian in Flatbush, Russian in Brighton Beach, Chinese in Sunset Park, and Dominican in Crown Heights all within an hour.

74707723-013b-4bf1-9a8b-c209dbf71984.fact: Bushwick is home to one of NYC's largest outdoor art projects — hundreds of massive murals cover buildings throughout the neighborhood, drawing tourists and inspiring street artists from around the world.
Bushwick was home to over 40 breweries in the 1880s, earning it the nickname 'the Brewers' Town' — German immigrants built an entire beer-making industry here before Prohibition killed it off.

47070afa-8638-4dfe-8df0-bdfa391f57b7.fact: The BQE (I-278) was Robert Moses's most controversial achievement — he demolished tens of thousands of apartments in the 1940s and 50s to ram it through Brooklyn and Queens.

7a830ac8-1cce-4a85-b3de-44d74900b9bc.fact: Bay Ridge has been home to successive waves of immigrants — Scandinavian sailors, Italian families, and now Arab-American communities have all left their mark on this waterfront neighborhood.

Saturday Night Fever (1977) was set and partly filmed in Bay Ridge — the neighborhood's character as a working-class enclave with aspirational energy was perfectly captured by John Travolta's Tony Manero.

ddc62c2f-dab2-48e0-918e-742e62e78ec1.fact: Bay Ridge has been home to successive waves of immigrants — Scandinavian sailors, Italian families, and now Arab-American communities have all left their mark on this waterfront neighborhood.

d125f801-5576-4e42-874c-a643a86019c0.fact: The St. George neighborhood at Staten Island's northern tip is undergoing a revival — the ferry terminal, a minor league baseball stadium, and the Snug Harbor Cultural Center are drawing visitors who have never explored the borough before.

The Staten Island Ferry is free and has been since 1997 — 70,000 riders a day make the crossing for work or pleasure, with one of the best views of the Statue of Liberty and Lower Manhattan skyline of any transit ride in the world.

db8c2d21-00cd-4508-945d-38fcd92367d2.fact: Brooklyn is technically its own city — the fourth largest in the US by population — that was absorbed into New York City in 1898 against the wishes of many residents who voted against consolidation.
